title: Atrial Fibrillation
authors: William H. Sauer, Jorge E. Romero, Paul C. Zei

items.title: Understanding Atrial Fibrillation's Impact on Stroke Risk
items.content: Atrial fibrillation significantly increases the risk of stroke, accounting for approximately 25% of all strokes. The disorganized atrial activation in AF leads to a higher likelihood of clot formation, which can embolize and cause ischemic events.
keyPoints: AF causes ~25% of all strokes.
Increased risk of stroke is a major consequence of AF.
Insertable cardiac monitors detect AF in up to 8.9% of patients within 6 months following cryptogenic stroke.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · Atrial Fibrillation | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)

items.title: Epidemiology and Risk Factors for Atrial Fibrillation
items.content: The prevalence of AF increases with age, affecting over 95% of patients older than 60 years. Key risk factors include hypertension, diabetes mellitus, cardiac disease, obesity, thyroid disease, and sleep-disordered breathing.
keyPoints: Prevalence >95% in patients >60 years old.
Lifetime risk for men aged 40 is ~25%.
Risk factors: hypertension, diabetes, cardiac disease, obesity, thyroid disease, sleep-disordered breathing.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · Atrial Fibrillation | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)

items.title: Classification and Progression of Atrial Fibrillation Episodes
items.content: AF episodes are classified based on duration: paroxysmal AF lasts ≤7 days, persistent AF lasts >7 days but <1 year, and long-standing persistent AF lasts >1 year. This classification reflects the progressive nature of AF.
keyPoints: Paroxysmal AF: ≤7 days.
Persistent AF: >7 days to <1 year.
Long-standing persistent AF: >1 year.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · Atrial Fibrillation | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)

items.title: Understanding Progression from Paroxysmal to Persistent AF
items.content: Atrial fibrillation (AF) progresses through stages based on the frequency and duration of episodes. Initially, patients experience paroxysmal AF due to ectopic depolarizations that trigger AF. Over time, with continued electrical, contractile, and structural remodeling, these episodes can become persistent as they no longer terminate spontaneously.
keyPoints: Ectopic depolarizations from the posterior wall of the left atrium and muscular tissue sleeves within the superior vena cava, coronary sinus, or vein of Marshall can trigger AF.
Paroxysmal AF involves episodes that are self-terminating but may progress to persistent AF as remodeling continues.
Persistent AF is characterized by episodes not terminating spontaneously due to ongoing electrical, contractile, and structural changes.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 2) | CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND MANIFESTATIONS

items.title: Diagnosing AF: Key ECG Findings and Clinical Manifestations
items.content: Atrial fibrillation is diagnosed through electrocardiogram (ECG) findings, which include the absence of organized atrial activity (no P wave) and an irregular ventricular response. Clinically, patients may experience symptoms related to rapid or slow ventricular rates, hemodynamic changes, cardioembolic events, and long-term cardiovascular impacts.
keyPoints: AF diagnosis is confirmed by ECG showing lack of organized atrial activity (no P wave) with an irregular ventricular response.
Clinical manifestations include palpitations due to variable cardiac displacement and contraction from irregular ventricular rates.
Many patients may be unaware of their irregular ventricular beating despite the presence of symptoms.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 2) | CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: TABLE 258-1 Categorization of Atrial Fibrillation (AF) by Clinical Temporal Characteristics and Associated Features
items.title: Impact of AF on Cardiac Output and Symptoms
items.content: Atrial fibrillation leads to a loss of atrial systole, causing irregular ventricular rates and variable stroke volumes. This results in decreased cardiac output, manifesting as exercise intolerance, fatigue, weakness, presyncope, or dyspnea. Patients with underlying cardiac conditions like hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, coronary artery disease, valvular disease, heart failure (both depressed and preserved ejection fraction), or amyloidosis are particularly vulnerable to exacerbated symptoms.
keyPoints: Loss of atrial systole impacts overall cardiac output
Symptoms include exercise intolerance, fatigue, weakness, presyncope, dyspnea
Patients with underlying cardiac disease (e.g., hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, coronary artery disease) are at higher risk
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · TABLE 258-1 Categorization of Atrial Fibrillation (AF) by Clinical Temporal Characteristics and Associated Features

items.subtopic: TABLE 258-1 Categorization of Atrial Fibrillation (AF) by Clinical Temporal Characteristics and Associated Features
items.title: Thromboembolic Risks and Cardiomyopathy in AF
items.content: In atrial fibrillation, blood stasis due to loss of atrial contraction can lead to thrombus formation, primarily in the left atrial appendage. This increases the risk of ischemic stroke. Recurrent thromboembolism may cause cognitive impairment and potentially increase dementia risk. Additionally, prolonged rapid ventricular rates from AF can result in tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy with reversible depressed left ventricular function once controlled.
keyPoints: Blood stasis promotes thrombus formation in the left atrial appendage
Increased risk of ischemic stroke and cognitive impairment
Prolonged rapid rates can lead to tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy, which is generally reversible
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · TABLE 258-1 Categorization of Atrial Fibrillation (AF) by Clinical Temporal Characteristics and Associated Features

items.title: When to Perform Emergent Cardioversion in AF
items.content: Emergent cardioversion is recommended for patients with acute onset of atrial fibrillation (AF) who exhibit significant hemodynamic compromise, pulmonary edema, or evidence of coronary ischemia.
keyPoints: Indications: Significant hemodynamic compromise, pulmonary edema, coronary ischemia
Methods: Electrical cardioversion with QRS synchronous shock in sedated patients; pharmacologic cardioversion using intravenous ibutilide
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · Atrial Fibrillation | CARDIOVERSION AND ANTICOAGULATION

items.title: Anticoagulation Strategy Before Cardioversion in AF >48 Hours or Unknown Duration
items.content: For patients with AF lasting more than 48 hours or of unknown duration, anticoagulation should be initiated and cardioversion deferred for at least 3 weeks after uninterrupted anticoagulation. Alternatively, a transesophageal echocardiography (TEE) or cardiac CT can be used to exclude left atrial appendage thrombus before cardioversion.
keyPoints: Duration >48 hours or unknown: Initiate anticoagulation
Cardioversion deferred for at least 3 weeks after uninterrupted anticoagulation
Alternative: Use TEE or cardiac CT to exclude thrombus
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · Atrial Fibrillation | CARDIOVERSION AND ANTICOAGULATION

items.title: Considerations for Cardioversion in Patients with Left Atrial Appendage Closure Devices
items.content: In patients with left atrial appendage closure devices, electrical cardioversion can be performed without oral anticoagulation if preprocedural TEE shows good device position, absence of device-related thrombus, and peri-device leak ≤5 mm.
keyPoints: Left atrial appendage closure devices: No need for oral anticoagulation
TEE criteria: Good device position, no device-related thrombus, peri-device leak ≤5 mm
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · Atrial Fibrillation | CARDIOVERSION AND ANTICOAGULATION

items.title: Achieving Acute Rate Control in Atrial Fibrillation
items.content: Acute rate control in atrial fibrillation (AF) aims to enhance diastolic filling time, thereby improving cardiac output and alleviating symptoms. This can be achieved using beta blockers, calcium channel blockers like verapamil or diltiazem, or digoxin, especially for patients with labile blood pressure or cardiomyopathy at risk of congestive heart failure.
keyPoints: Acute rate control improves diastolic filling time and cardiac output.
Beta blockers, calcium channel blockers (verapamil, diltiazem), and digoxin are used for acute rate control.
Digoxin is useful when beta blockers or calcium channel blockers alone are inadequate due to its synergistic effect.
Recent evidence suggests increased mortality with chronic use of digoxin.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · ACUTE RATE CONTROL | CHRONIC RATE CONTROL

items.title: Strategies for Chronic Rate Control in Atrial Fibrillation
items.content: Chronic rate control in AF aims to alleviate symptoms and prevent ventricular function deterioration. Beta blockers and calcium channel blockers are commonly used, with a target resting heart rate of <80 bpm that increases to <100 bpm during light exertion. If strict control is challenging, rates up to 110 bpm may be acceptable if asymptomatic and without ventricular dysfunction.
keyPoints: Chronic rate control targets symptom relief and prevention of ventricular deterioration.
Resting heart rate should be <80 beats/min, increasing to <100 beats/min with light exertion.
A lenient strategy (resting HR <110 bpm) is as effective as strict control for cardiovascular outcomes.
Consider catheter ablation and pacemaker implantation if pharmacologic therapy fails.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · ACUTE RATE CONTROL | CHRONIC RATE CONTROL

items.title: Use CHA2DS2-VASc Scoring for Stroke Risk Assessment in AF
items.content: The CHA2DS2-VASc scoring system is a critical tool used to estimate the risk of stroke in patients with atrial fibrillation. It helps determine whether anticoagulation therapy should be initiated, based on an individual's score.
keyPoints: Anticoagulation recommended for scores ≥1 unless the lone factor is female gender.
Annual stroke risk may reach nearly 20% without anticoagulation as CHA2DS2-VASc score increases.
CHA2DS2-VASc scoring system widely used in both the United States and Europe.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · STROKE PREVENTION IN ATRIAL FIBRILLATION (part 1)

items.title: Anticoagulation vs. Antiplatelet Therapy for Stroke Prevention
items.content: In non-valvular atrial fibrillation, anticoagulants like warfarin significantly reduce stroke risk compared to antiplatelet therapy or placebo.
keyPoints: Warfarin reduces annual stroke risk by 64% vs. placebo and 37% vs. antiplatelet therapy in nonvalvular AF.
Direct-acting anticoagulants (dabigatran, rivaroxaban, apixaban, edoxaban) are noninferior to warfarin with small superiority margins of 0.4–0.7% for mortality and stroke reduction.
Antiplatelet agents alone generally insufficient for stroke prevention in AF.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · STROKE PREVENTION IN ATRIAL FIBRILLATION (part 1)

items.title: Considerations for Subclinical AF and Stroke Risk
items.content: Subclinical atrial fibrillation, often asymptomatic and detected through continuous monitoring, increases stroke risk by a factor of 2.5.
keyPoints: Subclinical AF is associated with a 2.5-fold increased stroke risk.
Apixaban reduces the risk of stroke or systemic embolism in subclinical AF lasting 6 min to 24 h but raises major bleeding risk.
Continuous monitoring may be necessary for accurate stroke risk assessment in patients with borderline profiles.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · STROKE PREVENTION IN ATRIAL FIBRILLATION (part 1)

items.title: Direct-Acting Anticoagulants vs. Warfarin for Stroke Prevention in AF
items.content: Direct-acting anticoagulants such as dabigatran, rivaroxaban, and apixaban offer advantages over warfarin by achieving therapeutic anticoagulation more promptly without the need for frequent dose adjustments based on PT/INR monitoring. However, they require renal function consideration due to their excretion pathways.
keyPoints: Direct-acting agents achieve reliable anticoagulation promptly without requiring dosage adjustment based on blood tests.
Dabigatran, rivaroxaban, and apixaban have renal excretion and require dose adjustment for modest renal impairment, particularly in the elderly.
Major bleeding risk with warfarin is ~1% per year; direct-acting anticoagulants have a lower risk of intracranial bleeding compared to warfarin.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · STROKE PREVENTION IN ATRIAL FIBRILLATION (part 2)

items.title: Left Atrial Appendage Closure Devices for Stroke Prevention in AF
items.content: Left atrial appendage closure devices, such as the Watchman, provide stroke prevention comparable to warfarin and are associated with significant reductions in major bleeding events, particularly hemorrhagic strokes. These devices offer a noninferior alternative to both warfarin and direct-acting anticoagulants for patients at high risk of thromboembolism but also have serious bleeding risks.
keyPoints: Left atrial appendage closure devices provide stroke prevention comparable to warfarin with significant reductions in major bleeding, particularly hemorrhagic stroke, and all-cause mortality.
These devices are noninferior to direct-acting anticoagulants in preventing major AF-related cardiovascular, neurologic, and bleeding events.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · STROKE PREVENTION IN ATRIAL FIBRILLATION (part 2)

items.title: Indications for a Rhythm Control Strategy
items.content: A rhythm control strategy is typically chosen for patients with symptomatic paroxysmal AF, recurrent episodes of symptomatic persistent AF, difficult rate control, or AF that results in depressed ventricular function or aggravates heart failure. It's more favored in younger patients compared to sedentary or elderly ones.
keyPoints: Symptomatic paroxysmal AF
Recurrent episodes of symptomatic persistent AF
AF with difficult rate control
Depressed ventricular function due to AF
Aggravation of heart failure by AF
More likely favored in younger patients
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · RHYTHM CONTROL (part 1)

items.title: Role and Efficacy of Catheter Ablation in Rhythm Control
items.content: Catheter ablation is considered first-line therapy for maintaining sinus rhythm, especially in individuals with paroxysmal AF. It has shown superiority over medications for maintenance of sinus rhythm and benefits from an early rhythm control strategy.
keyPoints: First-line therapy for maintaining sinus rhythm
Superiority over antiarrhythmic drugs
Early rhythm control strategy reduces cardiovascular events
Beneficial in patients with symptomatic AF and end-stage heart failure
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · RHYTHM CONTROL (part 1)

items.title: Pharmacologic Therapy for Maintaining Sinus Rhythm
items.content: The goal of pharmacologic therapy is to maintain sinus rhythm or reduce episodes of AF. Risks and side effects are major considerations. Class I agents should be avoided in patients with coronary artery disease or heart failure, while class III agents have a risk of inducing QT prolongation.
keyPoints: Goal: Maintain sinus rhythm or reduce AF episodes
Class I agents (e.g., flecainide) avoid in coronary artery disease/heart failure
Class III agents (sotalol, dofetilide) ~3% risk of QT prolongation
Dofetilide requires hospital initiation with ECG monitoring
Amiodarone maintains sinus rhythm in ~two-thirds but has >40% toxicity risk
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · RHYTHM CONTROL (part 1)

items.title: Efficacy of Catheter Ablation vs. Antiarrhythmic Drugs
items.content: Catheter ablation is superior to antiarrhythmic drugs for patients with recurrent paroxysmal AF, especially those who have not responded well to drug therapy.
keyPoints: Superior efficacy of catheter ablation compared to antiarrhythmic drugs in previously untreated but recurrent paroxysmal AF.
Ablation is more effective than antiarrhythmic drugs for patients with recurrent AF despite drug treatment.
Long-term control of AF is more challenging in persistent and long-standing persistent AF due to extensive atrial abnormalities.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · RHYTHM CONTROL (part 2)

items.title: Pulsed-Field Ablation Characteristics
items.content: Pulsed-field ablation uses high-energy electric fields to target myocardium without damaging adjacent tissues, leveraging the unique sensitivity of myocardial cells.
keyPoints: Myocardial cells have electroporation thresholds of 268–375 V/cm.
Other tissue types (nerves, endothelium, vascular smooth muscle, blood cells) have electroporation thresholds >1600 V/cm.
Pulsed-field ablation lacks the ability to titrate and tailor energy delivery during ablation.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · RHYTHM CONTROL (part 2)

items.title: Outcomes of Catheter Ablation in Paroxysmal AF
items.content: Catheter ablation maintains sinus rhythm for over a year in approximately 80% of patients with paroxysmal AF after a single procedure.
keyPoints: Sinus rhythm maintained >1 year post-ablation in ~80% of patients with paroxysmal AF.
~90% achieve sinus rhythm after multiple procedures in some studies.
Pulsed-field ablation is noninferior to conventional thermal ablation regarding procedural success and safety at 1 year.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · RHYTHM CONTROL (part 2)

items.title: Ablation Strategies for Persistent AF
items.content: In patients with persistent atrial fibrillation (AF), combining catheter ablation of low-voltage myocardium and pulmonary vein isolation significantly improves outcomes. Additionally, using vein of Marshall ethanol infusion alongside catheter ablation yields better results compared to ablation alone.
keyPoints: Ablation of atrial low-voltage myocardium combined with pulmonary vein isolation enhances outcomes in persistent AF.
Combined catheter ablation and vein of Marshall ethanol infusion show superior outcomes over ablation alone in persistent AF.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · Catheter Variables

items.title: Complications Associated with Catheter Ablation
items.content: Catheter ablation carries a 2–7% risk of major complications, which are minimized by high-volume operators and centers. Complications include stroke (0.5–1%), cardiac tamponade (1%), phrenic nerve paralysis, bleeding from femoral access sites, fluid overload leading to heart failure, pulmonary vein stenosis, esophageal ulcers, and rare atrial-esophageal fistulas.
keyPoints: Major complication risk: 2–7% with catheter ablation.
Stroke incidence: 0.5–1%; cardiac tamponade: 1%.

Potential complications include phrenic nerve paralysis, bleeding from femoral access sites, fluid overload leading to heart failure, pulmonary vein stenosis, esophageal ulcers, and atrial-esophageal fistulas (<0.1%).
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · Catheter Variables

items.explanation: The passage states that AF significantly increases the risk of stroke.

items.question: Which of the following regions are known to produce ectopic depolarizations that may trigger atrial fibrillation (AF)?
options: Posterior wall of the left atrium, muscular tissue sleeves within the superior vena cava, coronary sinus, and vein of Marshall remnant.
Anterior wall of the right atrium, pulmonary veins, and interatrial septum.
Left ventricular outflow tract and aortic root.
Right ventricular free wall and tricuspid annulus.
items.explanation: The passage identifies these specific regions as sources of ectopic depolarizations that can trigger AF.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 2) | CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: What is the hallmark of persistent atrial fibrillation?
options: Episodes of AF that terminate spontaneously.
AF episodes not terminating spontaneously due to progressive remodeling.
items.explanation: Persistent AF is characterized by episodes that do not terminate spontaneously, as described in the passage.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 2) | CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: Which condition is most commonly associated with thrombus formation during AF?
items.explanation: Thrombus formation occurs primarily in the left atrial appendage during AF.

items.question: What is a potential consequence of recurrent thromboembolism to the brain in patients with AF?
items.explanation: Recurrent thromboembolism to the brain may result in cognitive impairment.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · TABLE 258-1 Categorization of Atrial Fibrillation (AF) by Clinical Temporal Characteristics and Associated Features

items.question: What is a possible outcome of tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy from AF?
options: Reversible myopathy once rates are controlled
items.explanation: Tachycardia-induced myopathy is generally reversible once ventricular rates are controlled.

items.question: In patients with new-onset AF and no significant hemodynamic compromise, what is the initial focus of therapy?
options: Control of ventricular rate to prevent hemodynamic sequelae
Pharmacologic cardioversion with ibutilide regardless of QT interval
items.explanation: In the hemodynamically stable patient with new-onset AF, therapy should focus on control of ventricular rate to prevent hemodynamic sequelae.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · Atrial Fibrillation | CARDIOVERSION AND ANTICOAGULATION

items.question: Which antiarrhythmic drug is typically used for pharmacologic cardioversion in new-onset AF?
items.explanation: Pharmacologic cardioversion is most typically achieved with the intravenous administration of the class III antiarrhythmic ibutilide.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · Atrial Fibrillation | CARDIOVERSION AND ANTICOAGULATION

items.question: When should anticoagulation be initiated before cardioversion if AF has been present for more than 48 hours?
options: 3 weeks before and a minimum of 4 weeks after cardioversion
items.explanation: One option to mitigate the risk related to cardioversion is to anticoagulate continuously for 3 weeks before and a minimum of 4 weeks after cardioversion.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · Atrial Fibrillation | CARDIOVERSION AND ANTICOAGULATION

items.question: Which imaging modality has been demonstrated to have excellent sensitivity and specificity for evaluating left atrial appendage thrombus?
options: Cardiac computed tomography (CT) angiography using delayed acquisition imaging
items.explanation: Cardiac CT angiography using delayed acquisition imaging has been demonstrated to have excellent sensitivity and specificity for evaluating left atrial appendage thrombus.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · Atrial Fibrillation | CARDIOVERSION AND ANTICOAGULATION

items.subtopic: ACUTE RATE CONTROL | CHRONIC RATE CONTROL
items.question: Which medication is typically avoided for chronic rate control in atrial fibrillation due to increased mortality with its use?
items.explanation: Recent evidence suggests increased mortality with the chronic use of digoxin, leading to a decline in its utilization.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · ACUTE RATE CONTROL | CHRONIC RATE CONTROL

items.subtopic: ACUTE RATE CONTROL | CHRONIC RATE CONTROL
items.question: What is an acceptable resting heart rate for patients with permanent atrial fibrillation under a lenient rate-control strategy?
items.explanation: A lenient rate-control strategy allows a resting heart rate of <110 beats/min.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · ACUTE RATE CONTROL | CHRONIC RATE CONTROL

items.subtopic: ACUTE RATE CONTROL | CHRONIC RATE CONTROL
items.question: Which combination is often used for chronic rate control in atrial fibrillation?
options: Beta blockers and calcium channel blockers
items.explanation: Beta-adrenergic blockers and calcium channel blockers are often used either alone or in combination for chronic rate control.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · ACUTE RATE CONTROL | CHRONIC RATE CONTROL

items.question: Which scoring system is used to estimate stroke risk in patients with atrial fibrillation?
items.explanation: The CHA2DS2-VASc scoring system is widely used to estimate stroke risk in patients with atrial fibrillation.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · STROKE PREVENTION IN ATRIAL FIBRILLATION (part 1)

items.question: What is the recommended anticoagulation strategy for nonvalvular AF patients with a CHA2DS2-VASc score of ≥1 in the United States and Europe?
options: No anticoagulation unless there are multiple risk factors
Anticoagulation unless the lone risk factor is female gender
items.explanation: Anticoagulation is recommended for patients with a CHA2DS2-VASc score of ≥1, except if the only risk factor is female gender.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · STROKE PREVENTION IN ATRIAL FIBRILLATION (part 1)

items.question: In nonvalvular AF, how does warfarin compare to antiplatelet therapy in reducing annual stroke risk?
options: Warfarin reduces the risk by 37% compared to placebo
Warfarin is less effective than antiplatelet therapy
Warfarin reduces the risk by 64% compared to placebo and by 37% compared to antiplatelet therapy
Antiplatelet therapy is more effective than warfarin
items.explanation: In nonvalvular AF, warfarin reduces annual stroke risk by 64% compared to placebo and by 37% compared to antiplatelet therapy.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · STROKE PREVENTION IN ATRIAL FIBRILLATION (part 1)

items.question: Which anticoagulant is required for patients with rheumatic mitral stenosis or mechanical heart valves?
items.explanation: Warfarin is required for patients with rheumatic mitral stenosis or mechanical heart valves.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · STROKE PREVENTION IN ATRIAL FIBRILLATION (part 1)

items.question: Which anticoagulant requires monitoring of PT/INR to adjust dose?
items.explanation: Warfarin requires monitoring of PT/INR to achieve and maintain a therapeutic effect.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · STROKE PREVENTION IN ATRIAL FIBRILLATION (part 2)

items.question: Which anticoagulant can be reversed by administration of vitamin K?
items.explanation: Vitamin K is used to reverse the effects of warfarin.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · STROKE PREVENTION IN ATRIAL FIBRILLATION (part 2)

items.question: Which agents are considered noninferior to direct-acting anticoagulants in preventing major AF-related cardiovascular, neurologic, and bleeding events?
items.explanation: Left atrial appendage closure devices are noninferior to direct-acting anticoagulants in preventing major AF-related events.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · STROKE PREVENTION IN ATRIAL FIBRILLATION (part 2)

items.question: Which patient population is most likely to benefit from a rhythm control strategy incorporating catheter ablation?
options: Patients with heart failure due to depressed left ventricular function
Patients with coronary artery disease without structural heart disease
items.explanation: A catheter ablation-based strategy provides an all-cause mortality benefit in patients with heart failure due to depressed left ventricular function.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · RHYTHM CONTROL (part 1)

items.question: What is the recommended approach for anticoagulation when initiating antiarrhythmic therapy?
options: Anticoagulation is not necessary if sinus rhythm is maintained.
An appropriate anticoagulation strategy similar to electrical cardioversion is recommended.
Only use anticoagulation if there are symptoms of AF.
Initiate anticoagulation only after confirming the absence of AF.
items.explanation: An appropriate anticoagulation strategy approach similar to electrical cardioversion is recommended, particularly at the time of initiation of therapy.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · RHYTHM CONTROL (part 1)

items.question: Which antiarrhythmic agent should be initiated only in a hospital with ECG monitoring due to its risk?
items.explanation: Dofetilide should be initiated only in a hospital with ECG monitoring due to its ~3% risk of inducing excessive QT prolongation and torsades des points.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · RHYTHM CONTROL (part 1)

items.question: Which antiarrhythmic drug is associated with increased mortality in patients with heart failure or long-standing persistent AF?
items.explanation: Dronedarone increases mortality in patients with heart failure or long-standing persistent AF.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · RHYTHM CONTROL (part 1)

items.question: Which treatment has shown superior efficacy compared to antiarrhythmic drug therapy for patients with recurrent paroxysmal AF?
items.explanation: Catheter ablation has superior efficacy compared to antiarrhythmic drug therapy for patients with recurrent paroxysmal AF.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · RHYTHM CONTROL (part 2)

items.question: What is the approximate percentage of patients requiring a repeat catheter ablation procedure due to gaps or new trigger sites?
items.explanation: Gaps in healed ablation areas or emergence of new trigger sites necessitate a repeat procedure in 10–30% of patients.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · RHYTHM CONTROL (part 2)

items.question: Which type of ablation uses electric fields generated by short pulses of high energy?
items.explanation: Pulsed-field ablation uses electric fields generated by short pulses of high energy.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · RHYTHM CONTROL (part 2)

items.question: In patients with paroxysmal AF, what percentage maintains sinus rhythm for >1 year after a single catheter ablation procedure?
items.explanation: Sinus rhythm is maintained for >1 year after a single ablation procedure in ~80% of patients with paroxysmal AF.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · RHYTHM CONTROL (part 2)

items.question: Which ablation target is associated with better outcomes in patients with persistent atrial fibrillation (AF) when combined with pulmonary vein isolation?
options: Ablation of the left atrial appendage
Combined catheter ablation and vein of Marshall ethanol infusion
Empiric ablation lines across regions of the atria
items.explanation: Treatment with combined catheter ablation and vein of Marshall ethanol infusion had better outcomes compared to catheter ablation alone in patients with persistent AF.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · Catheter Variables

items.question: What is a potential complication that can present weeks to months after pulmonary vein ablation?
items.explanation: Ablation within the pulmonary vein can lead to pulmonary vein stenosis, presenting weeks to months after the procedure.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · Catheter Variables

items.question: What is a rare complication that may occur following catheter ablation and present as endocarditis and stroke?
options: Fistula between the left atrium and esophagus
items.explanation: A fistula between the left atrium and esophagus can form after ablation, presenting as endocarditis and stroke.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · Catheter Variables

items.explanation: The passage mentions that uncontrolled AF can increase the risk of congestive heart failure.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · Atrial Fibrillation | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)

items.subtopic: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 2) | CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND MANIFESTATIONS
items.statement: AF is typically a progressive disease, although exceptions exist.
items.explanation: The passage mentions that AF tends to be progressive in most cases but acknowledges exceptions.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 2) | CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 2) | CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND MANIFESTATIONS
items.statement: Patients with AF always experience palpitations due to irregular ventricular rates.
items.explanation: The passage notes that many patients are unaware of the irregular ventricular beating for unknown reasons.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 2) | CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: TABLE 258-1 Categorization of Atrial Fibrillation (AF) by Clinical Temporal Characteristics and Associated Features
items.statement: Patients with AF and underlying cardiac disease may experience exacerbation of their condition.
items.explanation: The additional hemodynamic compromise from AF can exacerbate underlying cardiac diseases.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · TABLE 258-1 Categorization of Atrial Fibrillation (AF) by Clinical Temporal Characteristics and Associated Features

items.subtopic: TABLE 258-1 Categorization of Atrial Fibrillation (AF) by Clinical Temporal Characteristics and Associated Features
items.statement: Stiff left atrial syndrome is characterized by a compliant, non-fibrotic left atrium.
items.explanation: Stiff left atrial syndrome involves a noncompliant, fibrotic left atrium.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · TABLE 258-1 Categorization of Atrial Fibrillation (AF) by Clinical Temporal Characteristics and Associated Features

items.statement: Ibutilide is safe for use in patients with a baseline prolonged QT interval.
items.explanation: Ibutilide should be avoided in patients with baseline prolonged QT interval due to the risk of torsades des pointes.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · Atrial Fibrillation | CARDIOVERSION AND ANTICOAGULATION

items.statement: Electrical cardioversion can be performed without anticoagulation in low-risk patients if done within 48 hours of AF onset.
items.explanation: Cardioversion within 48 h of the onset of AF without TEE or cardiac CT is common practice in low-risk patients who have not been anticoagulated.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · Atrial Fibrillation | CARDIOVERSION AND ANTICOAGULATION

items.subtopic: ACUTE RATE CONTROL | CHRONIC RATE CONTROL
items.statement: Digoxin is preferred over beta blockers and calcium channel blockers for acute rate control due to its lack of negative inotropic effects.
items.explanation: Digoxin lacks the negative inotropic effect seen with calcium channel blockers and beta blockers, making it useful particularly in certain patients.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · ACUTE RATE CONTROL | CHRONIC RATE CONTROL

items.subtopic: ACUTE RATE CONTROL | CHRONIC RATE CONTROL
items.statement: A strict rate-control strategy is more effective than a lenient one for preventing cardiovascular death and hospitalization.
items.explanation: Both strategies are equally effective in terms of cardiovascular outcomes, though the lenient strategy is easier to achieve.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · ACUTE RATE CONTROL | CHRONIC RATE CONTROL

items.statement: Subclinical AF is associated with a decreased risk of stroke.
items.explanation: Subclinical AF is associated with an increased risk of stroke by a factor of 2.5.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · STROKE PREVENTION IN ATRIAL FIBRILLATION (part 1)

items.statement: Antiplatelet agents alone are generally sufficient for stroke prevention in nonvalvular AF.
items.explanation: Antiplatelet agents alone are generally not sufficient for stroke prevention in nonvalvular AF.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · STROKE PREVENTION IN ATRIAL FIBRILLATION (part 1)

items.statement: Direct-acting anticoagulants have a higher risk of intracranial bleeding compared to warfarin.
items.explanation: Direct-acting anticoagulants appear to have a lower risk of intracranial bleeding compared with warfarin.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · STROKE PREVENTION IN ATRIAL FIBRILLATION (part 2)

items.statement: Triple antithrombotic therapy is recommended for patients with high ischemic risk undergoing percutaneous coronary intervention.
items.explanation: Triple antithrombotic therapy, including a direct-acting anticoagulant, is considered in high-risk AF patients after percutaneous coronary intervention.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · STROKE PREVENTION IN ATRIAL FIBRILLATION (part 2)

items.statement: In older randomized trials, antiarrhythmic medications improved survival compared to a rate control strategy.
items.explanation: Older randomized trials showed that administration of antiarrhythmic medications did not improve survival or symptoms compared to a rate control strategy.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · RHYTHM CONTROL (part 1)

items.statement: Catheter ablation is considered first-line therapy for individuals with paroxysmal AF based on recent trial data.
items.explanation: Recent randomized trial data demonstrating superiority of ablation over medications for maintenance of sinus rhythm and benefits of an early rhythm control strategy support catheter ablation as first-line therapy.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · RHYTHM CONTROL (part 1)

items.statement: Pulsed-field ablation generates heat that can damage adjacent tissue.
items.explanation: Pulsed-field ablation specifically targets myocardium without generating heat or damaging adjacent tissue.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · RHYTHM CONTROL (part 2)

items.statement: Ablation is equally effective in patients with paroxysmal and persistent AF.
items.explanation: Ablation is less effective in patients with persistent AF, particularly long-standing persistent AF.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · RHYTHM CONTROL (part 2)

items.statement: The risk of major procedure-related complications from catheter ablation is consistently above 10%.
items.explanation: The risk of major procedure-related complications is 2–7%, not above 10%.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · Catheter Variables

items.statement: Complication rates for catheter ablation are lower with high-volume operators and centers.
items.explanation: Complications are lowest with high-volume operators and centers, suggesting better outcomes.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · Catheter Variables

items.assertion: AF is a major public health issue.
items.reason: It is the most common sustained arrhythmia and increases stroke risk significantly.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason explains why AF is a major public health issue.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · Atrial Fibrillation | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)

items.assertion: AF can be cured durably in a predictable fashion.
items.reason: The pathophysiology of AF is completely understood.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because there is no definitive cure for AF, and the reason is also false as the pathophysiology remains incompletely understood.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · Atrial Fibrillation | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)

items.subtopic: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 2) | CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND MANIFESTATIONS
items.assertion: AF episodes may progress to long-standing persistent AF.
items.reason: As electrical, contractile, and structural remodeling continues over time.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why AF can progress to long-standing persistent AF.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 2) | CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: TABLE 258-1 Categorization of Atrial Fibrillation (AF) by Clinical Temporal Characteristics and Associated Features
items.assertion: An increased risk of dementia in patients with AF may be due to recurrent thromboembolism.
items.reason: Chronic hypoperfusion in long-standing persistent atrial fibrillation contributes to this phenomenon.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but chronic hypoperfusion is not clearly established as a contributing factor.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · TABLE 258-1 Categorization of Atrial Fibrillation (AF) by Clinical Temporal Characteristics and Associated Features

items.assertion: Thromboembolism can occur after cardioversion if appropriate anticoagulation measures are not taken.
items.reason: Following conversion from prolonged AF to sinus rhythm, atrial mechanical function can be delayed for weeks (i.e., atrial stunning), such that thrombi can form even during sinus rhythm.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why thromboembolism can occur after cardioversion.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · Atrial Fibrillation | CARDIOVERSION AND ANTICOAGULATION

items.assertion: Patients with left atrial appendage closure devices do not require oral anticoagulation if preprocedural TEE shows good device position and absence of thrombus.
items.reason: Electrical cardioversion is feasible without the need for oral anticoagulation in these patients.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why oral anticoagulation may not be needed.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · Atrial Fibrillation | CARDIOVERSION AND ANTICOAGULATION

items.assertion: The CHA2DS2-VASc score is used to assess bleeding risk in patients with AF.
items.reason: The CHA2DS2-VASc scoring system estimates stroke risk, not bleeding risk.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the CHA2DS2-VASc score is for stroke risk. The reason is true but does not explain the assertion.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · STROKE PREVENTION IN ATRIAL FIBRILLATION (part 1)

items.assertion: Warfarin therapy has led to a lower rate of cardiovascular events or death than rivaroxaban in patients with rheumatic heart disease-associated AF.
items.reason: This is because warfarin is more effective for the prevention of valve thrombosis or thromboembolism in these patients.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · STROKE PREVENTION IN ATRIAL FIBRILLATION (part 1)

items.assertion: A rhythm control strategy is usually selected for patients with symptomatic paroxysmal AF.
items.reason: These patients often have frequent symptomatic episodes despite rate control, making a rhythm control strategy more beneficial.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why a rhythm control strategy is selected for these patients.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · RHYTHM CONTROL (part 1)

items.assertion: Class I sodium channel-blocking agents are suitable for all patients with AF.
items.reason: They have low efficacy in preventing or terminating AF episodes and can be proarrhythmic.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because Class I agents should be avoided in patients with coronary artery disease or heart failure due to their negative effects.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · RHYTHM CONTROL (part 1)

items.assertion: Pulsed-field ablation has shown to be noninferior to conventional thermal ablation in terms of freedom from a composite of initial procedural failure and other outcomes.
items.reason: The procedure demonstrated similar efficacy with respect to freedom from documented atrial tachyarrhythmia after a 3-month blanking period, antiarrhythmic drug use, cardioversion, or repeat ablation at 1 year.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · RHYTHM CONTROL (part 2)

items.assertion: Catheter ablation is less effective in patients with persistent AF due to extensive cardiac disease.
items.reason: More extensive ablation targeting areas that support reentry and/or AF maintenance is often required for these patients.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not directly explain why catheter ablation is less effective.
items.reference: Atrial Fibrillation · RHYTHM CONTROL (part 2)
